key_insight: The best inverse-solution method depends mainly on how the unknown is represented: grid-based fields favor adjoint optimization, while neural representations naturally favor PINNs.
engineering_relevance: For PDE-constrained inverse problems in mechanics and fluids, this comparison clarifies when to use adjoints for accuracy and when PINNs can reduce cost or better fit neural parameterizations.
method: Common formulation: same PDEs, observation models, regularization, optimizer, and precision for both methods.
Benchmarked on Burgers, Darcy permeability, Allen--Cahn reaction identification, and Navier--Stokes viscosity identification.
Tested a PINN-warm-started adjoint hybrid to combine fast initialization with adjoint-level accuracy.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports matched benchmark comparisons across four PDE inverse problems, showing performance differences tied to parameterization and time-dependent cost, with PINNs often reconstructing well at lower cost and warm-started adjoints improving efficiency.
watch_item: Results may depend strongly on the chosen parameterization, since the comparison is fair only when both methods are instantiated under identical formulations and optimization settings.
paper_title: Adjoint Method versus Physics-Informed Neural Networks in PDE-Constrained Inverse Problems
